full_response:
Assume we are given  
L = { (x1 ,y1 ), … , (xn ,yn ) } ,      all coordinates different.  

Part (d) of the problem already gave an O(n log n) sweep-line test that stops as soon as it meets ONE pair of points whose mutual distance is < 1.  
We now have to detect a THREE-clique, i.e. three sites whose pairwise
distance is < 1.

Key idea  
If a square of side ½ already contains three points, these three are certainly
mutually closer than 1, because the diagonal of the square is
√(½²+½²)=√½<1.  
Therefore every ½×½ square contains at most two points unless a valid
triple exists.  
With that observation we can keep all points seen so far in a
½×½ grid and, when a new point arrives, look only in a constant number of
neighboring squares; the total work per point is O(1).

Algorithm

Let cell(p) = (⌊2·p.x⌋ , ⌊2·p.y⌋) – the integer coordinates of the
½×½ square that contains p.
(Scaling by 2 turns side ½ into one integral unit.)

We scan the points from left to right (x-order) exactly as in part (d); only
points whose x–coordinate differs by <1 from the current sweep line are
kept in memory.

Data structures  
• A queue Q that stores the active points (their x is inside the 1-wide strip).  
• A hash table H whose keys are the integer pairs produced by cell(·);  
  for every key we remember the ≤ 2 points that are inside that cell.  
  (If a third point ever tries to enter the same cell we have already
  found a triple and can stop.)

Pseudocode

Triple-Within-One (L)
1. sort L increasingly by x–coordinate                        //  O(n log n)
2. Q ← empty queue    ,    H ← empty hash table
3. for p in L                                                 //  sweep line
       //  ─── forget sites that are too far to the left ───
       while Q is not empty and Q.front.x < p.x – 1
             q ← Q.pop_front()
             delete q from H[cell(q)]
       //  ─── look for a triangle that ends in p ───────────
       C ← {}           // candidates at distance < 1 from p
       for every cell (i ,j) with i ∈ {cx–2 … cx+2} and
                               j ∈ {cy–2 … cy+2} where (cx ,cy)=cell(p)
             for every point q in H[(i ,j)]
                    if ‖p–q‖ < 1
                         for every r in C        // |C| is O(1)
                               if ‖q–r‖ < 1     //  the missing edge
                                     return “YES – triangle found”
                         append q to C
       //  ─── insert p into the structures ─────────────────
       if H[cell(p)].size = 2                    //  third in a ½-cell
             return “YES – triangle found”
       insert p into H[cell(p)]    ;    Q.push_back(p)
4. return “NO – no such triple exists”

Correctness proof  
We prove that the algorithm outputs YES
iff three launch sites in L are mutually closer than 1.

(⇒)  If the algorithm outputs YES, it has exhibited three explicit
sites whose mutual distances were all verified to be < 1.

(⇐)  Suppose a triple T = {a,b,c} with pairwise distance < 1 exists.
Let c be the last of {a,b,c} processed by the sweep.  
At the moment c is examined both a and b are still inside the 1-wide
active strip (because their x differs from c.x by < 1)
and are therefore present in H in the 5×5 block of cells around cell(c).
Consequently the algorithm inspects the pairs (c,a) and (c,b)
(marking both of them “close”) and then checks the distance ‖a–b‖,
detecting the triangle and returning YES. ∎

Bounding the work per point

•  Each ½×½ cell contains at most 2 sites unless a triple has
   already been discovered.  
•  Every point looks only at the 5×5 = 25 surrounding cells; hence
   at most 50 stored points are inspected – a constant.  
•  Thus the inner part of the loop is O(1) and the whole sweep is Θ(n).

Total running time  
sorting      : Θ(n log n)  
sweep line   : Θ(n)  
overall      : Θ(n log n) worst-case.

Space  
The queue plus the hash table store only the active strip;
both hold O(n) points in the worst case ⇒ O(n) space.

\boxed{\text{By storing the points in a ½\times ½ grid during the usual
x-sweep, we can decide in }O(n\log n)\text{ time and }O(n)\text{ space
whether some three launch-sites are all within distance }1.}

Confidence: 0.39
